title: Equipment Review
narrationScript: At Level I you ran the penetrant line. At Level II you confirm it is fit to run, and your initials say so. This lesson walks the hardware end to end: the stationary line from precleaning through penetrant, wash, dryer, developer, and the darkened inspection booth, plus the aerosol kit and battery-powered UV-A lamp that carry the method into the field. Then it puts numbers on light, because lighting is a measured process variable. A calibrated radiometer must prove at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter of UV-A at the examination surface, after a mercury lamp has warmed up for five to ten minutes. A photometer must prove ambient white light at two footcandles or less in the booth, and at least one hundred footcandles at the surface for visible dye work. Finally, the physics and the physiology: a fluorescent indication emits yellow-green light near the eye's peak sensitivity in a dark-adapted booth, while visible red dye depends on reflected white light. If any number fails, parts wait.

sections.heading: The Hardware You Are Signing For
sections.body:
At Level I you operated this equipment under supervision. At Level II the equipment review changes meaning: before parts move, *you* confirm the system is fit to process them, and your initials on the shift record say so. That makes this review less about naming stations and more about knowing what each one must prove.

The **stationary line** runs the sequence you learned at Level I: a precleaning area (often separate vapor-degrease or solvent cleaning), the penetrant tank with its drain and dwell racks, an emulsifier station on Method B and D lines, the wash station — with a UV-A lamp mounted over the rinse on fluorescent lines so background removal can be watched as it happens — the recirculating hot-air dryer, the developer station, and the darkened inspection booth. Postcleaning closes the loop.

The **portable kit** compresses the same chemistry into aerosol cans — solvent cleaner/remover, penetrant, nonaqueous developer — plus lint-free wipes, a brush, and a battery-powered UV-A lamp for fluorescent field work. Same physics, same rules, same meters: a portable inspection answers to every lighting number a booth does.

sections.caption: The equipment under review — the stationary processing line, station by station, and the portable kit that carries the same process into the field.

sections.heading: Light Sources and Warm-Up Time
sections.body:
The classic UV-A source is the **mercury-vapor arc lamp** behind a deep-violet filter. The filter passes the UV-A band — roughly 320 to 400 nm, peaked near **365 nm** — and blocks both visible light and hazardous shorter wavelengths. Physics sets the warm-up rule: when the arc strikes, output climbs as the lamp heats and internal pressure builds, so the reading means nothing until the arc stabilizes — **commonly 5 to 10 minutes**. Never measure, and never inspect, under a mercury lamp that has not stabilized. And if a hot mercury lamp loses power, it will not restrike until it cools — plan on several dark minutes, not a flick of the switch.

**LED UV-A lamps** dominate new equipment: near-instant stable output, cooler running, long life. They are not automatically acceptable — aerospace practice requires LED sources certified for wavelength, irradiance, and beam profile (ASTM E3022 is the governing practice), and your procedure may list the approved models. LED or mercury, every lamp answers to the same radiometer.

**White-light sources** matter just as much: flood or task lighting that delivers evaluation-grade illumination at Type II benches, plus a hand-held white light for examining indication detail. And for any UV source, a **cracked or missing filter takes the lamp out of service on the spot**.

sections.heading: Methods of Measurement: Irradiance vs. Illuminance
sections.body:
Two meters, two different physical quantities. UV-A is invisible, so the eye is no reference: a **UV-A radiometer** measures **irradiance** — radiant power per unit area, in µW/cm² — through a sensor filtered to the UV-A band. White light is defined by what the eye sees, so a **white-light photometer** measures **illuminance** — in footcandles or lux — through a filter weighted to human visual response. Knowing which quantity you are reading is Level II knowledge: a photometer cannot vouch for a UV lamp, and a radiometer says nothing about booth ambient light.

Technique is unchanged from Level I, and it is everything:

- **Position** — sensor at the examination surface, facing the lamp, at the real working distance. Irradiance falls off roughly with the square of distance; many shops verify at 15 in. (38 cm) as a standard check.
- **Condition** — both meters on a traceable calibration cycle, commonly every 6 months. An expired sticker invalidates every reading the meter takes.
- **Timing** — verify UV-A intensity each shift or day under typical procedures, after a bulb or lamp change, after a lamp is dropped or relocated, and whenever output is suspect. Measure ambient light with the booth exactly as you inspect.
- **Record** — write the reading down. An unrecorded check did not happen.

sections.caption: The three numbers an equipment review must prove: UV-A irradiance at the surface and ambient white light for fluorescent work; white-light illuminance at the surface for visible work.
Lighting and measurement values an equipment review must confirm — typical practice; the written procedure governs.

LED UV-A lamps

Same radiometer checks as any other UV-A source

Dark adaptation

Procedure requirement on the inspector, repeated after each exit

sections.heading: Two Ways of Seeing: Physics and Physiology
sections.body:
**Visible (Type II) viewing is reflected-light seeing.** The white developer reflects the bench lighting; the red dye absorbs part of it. Contrast is therefore capped — the background can never be brighter than the light falling on it, and the indication can never be darker than zero reflection — which is why Type II work demands at least 100 fc of white light and full-color **photopic (cone) vision** to pull a red line out of a white field.

**Fluorescent (Type I) viewing is emitted-light seeing.** The dye absorbs invisible 365 nm UV-A and re-emits it as visible yellow-green light near 550 nm — fluorescence always shifts the energy to a longer wavelength. In a booth at 2 fc or less, the indication is essentially the only light source in view, so contrast is limited by booth darkness rather than by reflection. That is the physical root of fluorescent PT's sensitivity advantage.

The physiology is matched to the physics. Your retina's **cones** (photopic vision) peak near 555 nm; the **rods** that take over in dim light (scotopic vision) peak near 507 nm. Booth viewing sits between the two regimes — and yellow-green lands near the peak of both curves, which is precisely why penetrant dyes are formulated to emit there. Rods need time to come on line: allow **dark adaptation of at least 1 minute by typical procedure, longer after bright daylight**, and re-adapt after every trip out of the booth.

sections.title: Ultraviolet and the inspector's eye
sections.body: Never look into the beam of any UV source. A **cracked or missing filter** removes a lamp from service immediately — the filter is what blocks hazardous shorter-wavelength ultraviolet. Minimize prolonged UV-A exposure to bare skin. Wear **clear UV-absorbing safety glasses**: they protect the eye and suppress the haze created when UV makes the fluid of your own eye fluoresce. **Photochromic lenses are prohibited** — booth UV darkens them and steals the very signal you are there to see.

sections.heading: Readiness: Confirm Before the First Part
sections.body:
Equipment review ends as a gate, not a tour. Before the first part is processed, a Level II confirms — and can show records for — all of it:

- Lamps on, mercury arcs warmed and stabilized, **UV-A intensity measured at the examination surface and recorded**
- **Ambient white light verified** with the booth in its real inspection state — curtains, door, screens, indicator lights
- **White light verified at Type II benches** and available for examining indication detail
- Radiometer and photometer **calibration current**, stickers legible, records filed
- Dryer at its set temperature; gauges, timers, and pressure regulators working; tanks at working level
- Booth clean, surfaces dark and non-reflective, no fluorescent smears competing with indications
- The **daily system performance check** run on a known-defect panel under the same lights that will judge production parts

Any line that fails stops the process. You never inspect your way past a failed equipment check — every indication the system would have shown stays dark, and no one will know it was there.

figures.title: The PT equipment set: stationary line and portable kit
Pre-shift walkdown: five conditions to judge

interactive.title: Pre-Shift Walkdown: One Stop Condition
interactive.intro: You are walking down the fluorescent booth before first shift. Four of these five conditions pass review. Click the ONE condition that stops processing until it is corrected.

regions.label: UV-A lamp — hairline crack across the filter
regions.feedback: Correct. A cracked filter takes the lamp out of service immediately, no matter what the radiometer says — the deep-violet filter is what keeps hazardous shorter-wavelength ultraviolet out of the booth, and a crack also leaks visible light that raises background. Tag it out and swap the lamp before any part is processed.

regions.label: Warm-up timer — mercury arc on for 12 minutes
regions.feedback: This passes. Twelve minutes exceeds the commonly required 5–10 minute warm-up, so the arc has stabilized and intensity readings taken now actually mean something. The condition that fails review is measuring or inspecting before stabilization.

regions.label: Radiometer — 1,210 µW/cm² at the part surface, calibration current
regions.feedback: This passes. The sensor is at the examination surface at the working distance, the reading clears the typical 1,000 µW/cm² minimum, and the calibration is in date — this is exactly what a defensible UV-A reading looks like. Record it.

regions.label: Ambient photometer — 1.6 fc with the curtain sealed
regions.feedback: This passes. Ambient white light is under the typical 2 fc (about 20 lux) limit, and it was measured with the booth in its real inspection state — curtain sealed, exactly as parts will be viewed.

regions.label: Clear UV-absorbing safety glasses on the bench
regions.feedback: These are the right lenses. Clear UV-absorbing glasses protect the inspector's eyes and cut the haze from fluorescence inside the eye itself. The lenses that fail a walkdown are photochromic ones — booth UV darkens them and robs the inspector of signal.

checkQuestions.question: Which instrument and unit correctly verify UV-A intensity at the examination surface?
options: A white-light photometer reading footcandles
A UV-A radiometer reading microwatts per square centimeter (µW/cm²)
A lux meter reading total lumens from the lamp
A densitometer reading optical density of the filter
checkQuestions.explanation: UV-A is invisible, so it is measured radiometrically — radiant power per unit area in µW/cm² — with a sensor filtered to the UV-A band. Photometers and lux meters read eye-weighted visible light and cannot vouch for a UV source.

checkQuestions.question: A mercury-vapor UV lamp is switched on, and 60 seconds later the radiometer reads 700 µW/cm² at the surface. What is the correct interpretation?
options: The bulb has failed and must be replaced before the shift starts
The booth ambient light is too high and is suppressing the reading
The radiometer is out of calibration and should be exchanged
The arc has not finished warming up — wait the procedure's warm-up time, commonly 5–10 minutes, and re-measure
checkQuestions.explanation: A mercury arc's output climbs as the lamp heats and internal pressure builds, so a reading taken cold is meaningless. Condemn nothing until the lamp has stabilized — and remember a hot lamp that loses power must cool before it will restrike.

checkQuestions.question: Why are fluorescent penetrant dyes formulated to emit yellow-green light?
options: Yellow-green falls near the peak sensitivity of human vision — both cone and rod response — so the dim emitted light is seen most easily
Yellow-green light penetrates deeper into tight cracks than other colors
Yellow-green is the natural color of UV-A radiation once it reflects off developer
Yellow-green dyes are the only ones that survive dryer temperatures
checkQuestions.explanation: Photopic (cone) vision peaks near 555 nm and scotopic (rod) vision near 507 nm; yellow-green emission near 550 nm lands close to the peak of both curves, so a faint indication in a darkened booth produces the strongest possible visual response. UV-A itself is invisible — the dye converts it to visible light.

checkQuestions.question: A Type II visible-dye examination is set up on a bench where the photometer reads 40 fc at the part. What does typical practice require?
options: Proceed — visible dye work has no lighting requirement
Darken the bench below 2 fc so contrast improves
Raise the white light to at least 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) at the examination surface before evaluating
Supplement the bench with a UV-A lamp to make up the difference
checkQuestions.explanation: Visible red-on-white contrast is reflected-light seeing and needs evaluation-grade illumination — typically a minimum of 100 fc (about 1,000 lux) at the surface. Darkness helps fluorescent work, not visible dye, and UV-A cannot substitute for white light.

checkQuestions.question: During the walkdown you find the radiometer's calibration sticker expired last week, although today's booth reading 'passes.' What is the equipment status?
options: Acceptable — the meter was reading correctly as recently as last week
Not acceptable — readings from an out-of-calibration meter are invalid, so readiness cannot be shown until a calibrated meter proves the numbers
Acceptable as long as the reading exceeds the minimum by a comfortable margin
Acceptable for visible-dye work, since that method does not use the radiometer
checkQuestions.explanation: Light meters live on a traceable calibration cycle, commonly every 6 months. Once calibration lapses, every reading the meter takes is unsupported — the lighting check cannot be credited, and processing waits until a calibrated instrument proves the values.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline, Level II §1.3 and light measurement topics (equipment; white light and UV intensity; physics and physiological differences)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (lighting values and process control, general reference)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (light source warm-up and examination conditions, general reference)
ASTM E3022, emission requirements for LED UV-A lamps used in fluorescent PT and MT (general reference)
